does: This paper proposes VGG-Flow, a gradient-matching method for aligning pretrained flow matching models with human preferences through efficient finetuning. The core contribution is grounded in optimal control theory: the method matches the optimal difference between a finetuned and pretrained velocity field to the gradient field of a value function derived from a reward model. By leveraging first-order reward information and heuristic value function initialization, VGG-Flow enables fast adaptation with limited computational resources. The authors demonstrate the approach on Stable Diffusion 3, showing that the method achieves effective alignment while preserving the probabilistic properties of the original pretrained model, addressing a key limitation of existing flow matching alignment techniques that either suffer from slow adaptation or degrade the model's prior distribution.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of aligning generative models with human preferences, extending beyond prior methods for diffusion models to the increasingly popular class of flow matching models. It connects to optimal control theory applied to neural network training, drawing inspiration from how value gradients guide policy updates in reinforcement learning. The paper responds to limitations in existing flow matching alignment approaches that struggle to balance adaptation efficiency with prior preservation—a problem well-studied in the broader alignment literature but underexplored for flow models. The use of reward models and gradient-based finetuning relates to recent work on preference-driven adaptation of generative models, while the theoretical grounding in optimal control represents a more principled approach than prior heuristic alignment methods.
enables: This work opens directions for efficient alignment of other flow-based generative models beyond text-to-image tasks, including audio, video, and multimodal generation. The optimal control framework could inspire further theoretical analysis of alignment-prior preservation tradeoffs and potentially lead to principled methods for other generative architectures. By demonstrating that value gradients can guide model adaptation, the paper suggests avenues for combining reinforcement learning techniques with modern generative models more broadly. The computational efficiency gains suggest practical applications in iterative human-in-the-loop alignment, where models must be frequently updated with new preference data. Future work might explore stronger guarantees on prior preservation, extend the method to other reward specifications beyond scalar rewards, or develop multi-objective variants that balance multiple human preferences simultaneously.
